source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A glass hemisphere with a radius of $R = 0.128 \text{ m}$ is given. The straight line passing through the center $O$ of the sphere and perpendicular to the flat surface is its principal axis. A thin, luminous bar $AB$ (with $B$ closer to the center $O$) with length $l = 0.020 \text{ m}$ is placed along the principal axis. If an eye is placed near the principal axis and looks towards the flat surface of the hemisphere (as shown in Figure), two dim images of the luminous bar can be seen (in addition, there may be weaker images that need not be considered). When the luminous bar moves along the principal axis, these two images also move along the principal axis. Now adjust the position of the luminous bar to make its two images coincide. At this time, the distance from the near end $B$ of the luminous bar to the center $O$ is $a_2 = 0.020 \text{ m}$. Use the given data to calculate the refractive index $n$ of the glass hemisphere (only paraxial rays are considered in the calculation).

image_dependency: Question explicitly says 'as shown in the figure'
Optical setup depends on spatial relations among hemisphere, principal axis, luminous bar, and viewing direction
multi_step: Requires modeling two separate images of the bar and using the condition that they coincide
Needs paraxial optics reasoning for a glass hemisphere rather than direct lookup
Multiple given quantities (R, l, a2) must be combined to solve for refractive index n
verifiability: answer_type is numeric and answer_text is a specific value: 1.6
Multiple-choice options are provided, enabling straightforward final-answer checking
registry_passed=true, with question, image, and answer all present

risk_flags: physics derivation may be sensitive to interpretation of the two image paths

decision_reasons: Question, image, and numeric answer are all present, so the core semantics are intact.
Asset registry passed with no recorded issues, indicating good sample completeness.
Initial scores are strong for image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability.
Although the physics setup is figure-dependent and interpretation-sensitive, the required figure is available, so this is not a blocking risk.
